     7	# Feature: DDx Agent Service (consumer of ddx-agent contract)
     8	
     9	**ID:** FEAT-006
    10	**Status:** In Progress (migrating to ddx-agent CONTRACT-003)
    11	**Priority:** P0
    12	**Owner:** DDx Team
    13	
    14	## Overview
    15	
    16	DDx invokes LLMs through the `ddx-agent` module, defined by
    17	**CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service** in the `~/Projects/agent` repo
    18	(`docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md`).
    19	
    20	That contract is the entire execution boundary. DDx exposes the public
    21	`ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` layers and calls the upstream
    22	`agentlib.DdxAgent` interface for the actual agent invocation. Upstream
    23	diagnostic/status commands may remain mounted under `ddx agent`, but
    24	`ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, and `ddx agent execute-loop` are not
    25	public workflow commands.
    26	
    27	## DDx-side responsibilities
    28	
    29	DDx owns the bead-driven workflow surface. It does NOT own:
    30	- Harness orchestration (claude, codex, opencode, pi, gemini, native)
    31	- Provider routing
    32	- Model catalog
    33	- Compaction
    34	- Tool registration
    35	- Session-log shape
    36	
    37	Those all live inside ddx-agent per CONTRACT-003.
    38	
    39	DDx owns:
    40	
    41	- **Bead-driven invocation.** `ddx try`, `ddx work`, and the server's
    42	  queue-drain worker translate bead state into `ExecuteRequest` values and
    43	  surface results back into the bead tracker. The queue-drain worker
    44	  defaults to a long-running 30s `--poll-interval` (ddx-dc157075):
    45	  workers stay alive across empty polls and surface "no ready work" as a
    46	  transient `idle` substate. Operators opt out with `--once` (one-shot)
    47	  or `--poll-interval=0` (legacy drain-and-exit).
    48	- **Bead-attempt orchestration.** Worktree creation, base-revision pinning,
    49	  result landing (merge / preserve / no-changes), gate evaluation, evidence
    50	  bundle capture. The agent provides the LLM execution; DDx provides the
    51	  git-aware orchestration.
    52	- **Structured prompt handoff.** Review, grading, and other prompt-bearing
    53	  invocations use the bounded assembly invariants from FEAT-022 and the
    54	  caller-facing structured evidence envelope described in TD-033.
    55	  Review-mode sessions are launched in TD-033's no-tool reviewer mode so the
    56	  agent reasons over the supplied evidence instead of discovering new context
    57	  at runtime.
    58	- **Evidence and session capture.** DDx writes `.ddx/executions/<id>/`
    59	  bundles with prompts, manifests, and result artifacts. The agent's session
    60	  log path (returned in `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`) is captured into
    61	  the bundle.
    62	- **Power policy at the request level.** DDx selects requested agent power
    63	  bounds, effort, and permissions per bead attempt based on bead metadata, user
    64	  input, and prior attempt outcomes. The agent receives `MinPower` and
    65	  optionally `MaxPower` on `ExecuteRequest` and performs the routing.
    66	- **Agent passthrough constraints.** DDx may accept explicit `--harness`,
    67	  `--provider`, and `--model` values from the operator and pass them unchanged
    68	  to the agent. DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, rewrite, or reason about
    69	  these fields; they are opaque constraints for the agent to interpret.
    70	- **Retry policy.** DDx owns the bead retry loop because DDx owns the evidence
    71	  needed to decide whether an attempt succeeded: commits, merge/preserve
    72	  result, no-changes rationale, post-run gates, review verdicts, cooldowns,
    73	  and prior run metadata. On retry, DDx may raise `MinPower`. The agent owns
    74	  how those power bounds map to a concrete model/provider.
    75	
    76	## Power Intent
    77	
    78	DDx does not route. DDx chooses request-level power bounds and sends them to
    79	`agentlib.DdxAgent.Execute` as `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`; the agent
    80	resolves harness, provider, endpoint, model, health, quota, fallback, and route
    81	errors.
    82	
    83	Power is an abstract integer scale owned by the agent contract. DDx treats
    84	`MinPower`/`MaxPower` as bounds on that scale, not as model identities. For
    85	example, the agent may report:
    86	
    87	```text
    88	running with qwen 3.6-27b (power 10)
    89	```
    90	
    91	DDx records requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower` and the actual model/power returned
    92	by the agent. DDx can use that evidence on a later retry to raise `MinPower`,
    93	but it still does not choose the next model.
    94	
    95	The agent also exposes its available model/power catalog. DDx may read that
    96	catalog to choose a `MinPower` threshold for "top model only" retries, for
    97	example by requesting a lower bound at or above the lowest power among the
    98	current top models. DDx must not use the catalog to pin a concrete
    99	model/provider on the normal execution path.
   100	
   101	DDx may also pass request facts such as estimated prompt size, whether tools are
   102	required, permissions, timeout values, and effort/reasoning intent. These facts
   103	describe the work; they do not select a model.
   104	
   105	## Non-Bead Invocation Intent
   106	
   107	Not every layer-1 invocation comes from a bead. Artifact generation,
   108	artifact regeneration, prompt comparison skills, and diagnostics can call
   109	`ddx run` directly. For those calls DDx may choose work intent from the
   110	artifact or operation being performed:
   111	
   112	- `MinPower` / optional `MaxPower` derived from the artifact's generator,
   113	  media type, size, risk, or explicit operator flags
   114	- permissions needed for the operation, such as read-only, workspace-write, or
   115	  network access
   116	- timeout and effort/reasoning intent
   117	- metadata such as `produces_artifact`, artifact id, execution definition id,
   118	  and governing document ids
   119	- review-mode prompt envelopes that are explicitly no-tool and assembled per
   120	  TD-033 / FEAT-022
   121	
   122	These are DDx-owned work facts, not routing decisions. DDx must not translate an
   123	artifact key, media type, generator name, or execution definition into a concrete
   124	harness/provider/model. The only concrete route-like fields DDx may send are
   125	operator-supplied passthrough constraints, carried unchanged in the passthrough
   126	envelope described below.
   127	
   128	## Agent Passthrough Constraints
   129	
   130	`--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are permitted on `ddx run`, `ddx try`,
   131	and `ddx work` only as passthrough fields. DDx carries them in one narrow
   132	request envelope and sends them to `agentlib.DdxAgent.Execute` unchanged.
   133	
   134	These fields must not leak into DDx routing policy:
   135	
   136	- no DDx-side validation against harness/provider/model catalogs
   137	- no preflight `ResolveRoute`
   138	- no provider/model fallback or substitution
   139	- no retry-policy branches that inspect the string values
   140	- no config-driven default harness/provider/model selection
   141	- no worker columns or queue selection logic that treat these as DDx concepts
   142	
   143	If an operator supplies passthrough constraints and DDx later retries with a
   144	higher `MinPower`, DDx keeps the passthrough values unchanged. The agent
   145	decides whether the power bounds and passthrough constraints are compatible and
   146	reports the actual model/power or a typed error. DDx records the requested
   147	passthrough values for audit, but it does not use them to select a route.
   148	
   149	When hard passthrough pins make the requested power bounds unsatisfiable, DDx
   150	must stop with a typed terminal classification such as
   151	`blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or `agent_power_unsatisfied`. DDx records
   152	the requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`, passthrough envelope, and agent-supplied
   153	evidence, then reports operator action required. DDx must not remove pins,
   154	choose alternatives, call `ResolveRoute` to work around the conflict, or loop on
   155	higher `MinPower` values.
   156	
   157	The only DDx code allowed to touch raw passthrough fields is the CLI parser, the
   158	single passthrough/intent value, Execute request construction, execution-record
   159	persistence, and human-facing evidence rendering. Retry, queue selection, work
   160	scheduling, catalog threshold helpers, and config defaulting must carry the
   161	opaque value without reading `Harness`, `Provider`, or `Model`.
   162	
   163	`ResolveRoute` is not part of normal execution. It is allowed only for
   164	operator-facing status/debug surfaces such as route-status and provider
   165	diagnostics. A `RouteDecision` returned by `ResolveRoute` must never be
   166	re-injected into `Execute`.
   167	
   168	`agent.routing.profile_ladders`, `agent.routing.model_overrides`,
   169	`agent.routing.profile_priority`, and the `cheap`/`standard`/`smart` profile
   170	taxonomy are not DDx execution policy. Configs or tests that rely on DDx-managed
   171	ladders, profiles, or model overrides are migration debt.
   172	
   173	## Catalog and Status Boundary
   174	
   175	Model/power catalog rendering is observability. Catalog status surfaces may show
   176	model names, providers, powers, and route candidates returned by the agent for
   177	debugging. Retry threshold policy may consume only abstract power numbers from
   178	the catalog to compute `MinPower` thresholds. Neither status nor retry code may
   179	return or mutate concrete harness/provider/model pins for execution.
   180	
   181	## Session Log Envelope Boundary
   182	
   183	The upstream agent owns the native session-log schema and any harness-specific
   184	details inside it. DDx owns only the envelope around that log:
   185	
   186	- request id, layer, parent run id, bead id or produced artifact id
   187	- requested `MinPower` / `MaxPower` and opaque passthrough constraints
   188	- actual model and actual power returned by the agent
   189	- pointer or copied attachment for `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`
   190	- DDx-owned attempt outcome, merge/preserve outcome, gates, and evidence refs
   191	
   192	DDx may render or link the session log for humans, but normal execution policy
   193	must not parse the inner session log to infer routing, provider health, model
   194	fallbacks, or retry destinations. Typed agent response fields and DDx-owned
   195	attempt evidence are the policy inputs.
   196	
   197	## Layer Ownership Migration Table
   198	
   199	FEAT-006 is deliberately narrow after the three-layer refactor. It describes
   200	how DDx consumes the upstream agent contract and how layer-1 requests are
   201	formed; it does not own worktree, queue, or CLI namespace design.
   202	
   203	| Area | Owner after migration |
   204	| --- | --- |
   205	| CONTRACT-003 `agentlib.DdxAgent` boundary, `ExecuteRequest` / `ExecuteResponse` consumption, `MinPower` / `MaxPower`, actual model/power recording | Stays in FEAT-006 |
   206	| Opaque passthrough envelope for `--harness`, `--provider`, `--model` and the rule that DDx must not route on those fields | Stays in FEAT-006 |
   207	| Non-bead layer-1 invocation intent: artifact-keyed power bounds, permissions, timeout, effort, and metadata | Stays in FEAT-006 |
   208	| Session-log envelope and pointer/copy capture around the agent-owned inner log | Stays in FEAT-006 |
   209	| Worktree creation, base revision pinning, bead prompt resolution, result landing, preserve refs, post-run gates, evidence bundles | Moves to FEAT-010 layer 2 (`ddx try`) |
   210	| Unified `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` substrate, layer metadata, child/parent run links, migration from `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/` | Moves to FEAT-010 |
   211	| Queue iteration, no-progress detection, retry scheduling, cooldowns, stop conditions, loop records | Moves to FEAT-010 layer 3 (`ddx work`) |

   215	### Conflict-recovery structured outcomes (bead ddx-0097af14)
   216	
   217	Before parking a bead on `land_conflict` (or on `execution_failed` when the
   218	agent produced a commit before failing), the `ddx work` drain now attempts to reuse
   219	the preserved iteration ref rather than discarding the agent's work:
   220	
   221	1. **3-way ort auto-merge** (`git merge --no-ff -s ort -X ours`). If clean,
   222	   the bead closes as `success`.
   223	2. **Focused conflict-resolve agent** (if `ConflictResolver` is configured on
   224	   the worker). If clean, the bead closes as `success`.
   225	3. **Park with structured outcome.** Two new statuses replace the generic
   226	   reopen when both recovery paths fail:

   229	| --- | --- | --- |
   230	| `land_conflict_unresolvable` | `land-conflict-unresolvable` | All auto-recovery failed; retryable after 15-min cooldown. |
   231	| `land_conflict_needs_human` | `land-conflict-needs-human` | Focused-resolve agent returned BLOCKING; human input required. |

    10	---
    11	# Feature: Prompt Evidence Assembly and Size Invariants
    12	
    13	**ID:** FEAT-022
    14	**Status:** Implemented
    15	**Priority:** P1
    16	**Owner:** DDx Team
    17	
    18	## Overview
    19	
    20	DDx constructs prompts for LLM agents from multiple evidence sources: bead
    21	metadata, governing documents, git diffs, comparison-arm outputs, user-supplied
    22	prompt files, and inline request bodies. Today these assembly paths inline
    23	content without size bounds, produce oversize prompts, and in the reviewer's
    24	case trigger silent provider-side failures that cascade into redone primary
    25	work and wasted tokens.
    26	
    27	This feature establishes a single repo-wide invariant: **no prompt DDx
    28	constructs or accepts may have unbounded size.** It specifies the shared
    29	primitives every prompt-building call site must use, the two supported
    30	assembly strategies, the failure semantics when inputs exceed caps, and the
    31	telemetry needed to observe the system.
    32	
    33	## Problem Statement
    34	
    35	**Current situation:**
    36	
    37	- The post-merge bead reviewer (`BuildReviewPrompt`) inlines full governing
    38	  documents and full `git show` diffs without a cap. Large diffs push the
    39	  prompt past the reviewer model's context window; some providers return a
    40	  hard error, some return zero bytes. DDx surfaces these as `review-error`,
    41	  fails to close the bead, and the next worker redoes the primary task —
    42	  burning the same large token budget 3–4 times per bead before an operator
    43	  intervenes.
    44	- The comparison grader (`buildGradingPrompt`) has the same failure shape:
    45	  per-arm outputs and diffs are inlined without a cap.
    46	- A second review-prompt assembler lives in `cmd/bead_review.go` as
    47	  duplicated code, with divergent escape semantics from the agent-side
    48	  assembler.
    49	- Five call sites (`runner.go`, `compare_adapter.go:181`,
    50	  `compare_adapter.go:614`, `service_run.go:83`, `execute_bead.go:1291`)
    51	  read `--prompt` files with `os.ReadFile` and no cap. A 100 MB prompt file
    52	  reaches the provider unchecked.
    53	- Three inline prompt-ingress paths (`/api/agent/run` body `Prompt`,
    54	  `DDX_AGENT_PROMPT` env in exec definitions, docgraph prompt metadata)
    55	  accept prompt bodies with no cap.
    56	- Server text egress (MCP, REST, GraphQL) returns full document, diff,
    57	  session, and persona payloads without caps. Any of these responses may
    58	  later flow back into a prompt.
    59	- No DDx call site has section-level telemetry on prompt assembly. When
    60	  reviews fail, diagnosis today requires per-execution bundle archaeology.
    61	
    62	**Desired outcome:** Every LLM prompt DDx constructs, accepts, or emits
    63	through text-returning server endpoints is bounded by explicit caps.
    64	Prompt-assembly call sites use shared primitives that enforce caps with
    65	clear truncation markers. Oversize inputs fail fast with actionable errors
    66	rather than silently corrupting provider calls. Operators see per-section
    67	byte accounting on every review and grading attempt.
    68	
    69	## Requirements
    70	
    71	### Functional
    72	
    73	**Shared evidence primitives**
    74	
    75	1. **Single evidence-assembly package.** DDx provides one package exporting
    76	   the primitives every prompt-building call site must use. The package
    77	   covers, at minimum, these capabilities:
    78	   - Byte-size caps for inlined files, diffs, governing documents, and
    79	     total prompts, each with per-harness override resolution.
    80	   - Clamped file read (returns content plus amount truncated).
    81	   - Clamped text output (bounded length with a canonical truncation
    82	     marker).
    83	   - Section fitting under a total byte budget with priority-ordered
    84	     inclusion. Default behavior is line-based and type-agnostic;
    85	     markdown-aware heading extraction is an optional secondary mode.
    86	   - Diff clamping and diff decomposition. The decomposer returns file
    87	     inventory, per-file stat lines, and hunk headers so large diffs
    88	     can degrade to stat + hunk-headers only.
    89	   - Two strategy helpers, one for inline-with-cap assembly and one for
    90	     ref-only metadata assembly (see §2).
    91	   - A canonical truncation-marker string so downstream parsers
    92	     recognize truncation uniformly across call sites.
    93	
    94	   Specific Go identifiers, type shapes, and constant values are defined
    95	   in the solution design document for this feature, not in this spec.
    96	
    97	1a. **Cap defaults and configurability.** Byte-size caps have conservative
    98	    defaults shipped in the binary. Project-level overrides are expressed
    99	    in `.ddx/config.yaml` under a dedicated evidence-caps section;
   100	    per-harness overrides resolve from the same configuration file.
   101	    Defaults and override precedence are defined in the solution design
   102	    for this feature. The invariant is that every cap is configurable at
   103	    the project level without a rebuild; specific numeric defaults are
   104	    not frozen by this spec.
   105	
   106	2. **Two supported assembly strategies.** Each call site must adopt one:
   107	   - **Ref-only metadata** — the primary bead prompt's pattern. Paths and
   108	     titles only; agent reads file content via its own tools. Appropriate
   109	     when the agent has filesystem access and the prompt does not need to
   110	     guarantee the agent saw specific content.
   111	   - **Inline-with-cap** — reviewer and grader's pattern. Content is
   112	     inlined with bounded size, priority-ordered sections, and truncation
   113	     markers. Appropriate when the call must guarantee the agent saw
   114	     specific evidence.
   115	
   116	3. **Repo-wide invariant (no unbounded prompts).** No code path may produce
   117	   a value that ends up as an agent-invocation prompt, as a text response
   118	   from a server handler that carries library, execution, diff, session,
   119	   or persona content, or as an inline prompt body accepted through any
   120	   ingress surface, without going through an evidence-package primitive.
   121	   Exceptions require an explicit `evidence:allow-unbounded` source
   122	   annotation with a justifying comment and are enforced by lint (see
   123	   Non-Functional §Lint). Static/literal prompt fragments compiled into
   124	   the binary are exempt; the invariant governs dynamic assembly from
   125	   runtime inputs, not constant strings.
   126	
   127	3a. **Static-prompt minimum-prompt rule.** Every static instruction string
   128	    DDx embeds in an LLM prompt MUST declare its load-bearing guardrails
   129	    inline as a Go comment list above the constant, one bullet per
   130	    guardrail with a pointer to the failure mode it prevents. Edits MUST
   131	    preserve every listed guardrail. New guardrails MUST be added to the
   132	    list. A regression test MUST assert each listed guardrail still
   133	    appears in the rendered prompt. This rule applies to STATIC prompt
   134	    bodies; dynamic evidence egress is covered by the §5–§7 cap
   135	    primitives and is unaffected.
   136	
   137	**Bounded prompt construction**
   138	
   139	4. **Single review assembler.** Review prompts are assembled by exactly one
   140	   function (`agent.BuildReviewPrompt`). Any duplicate review-prompt
   141	   assembler in the CLI command layer is collapsed into this function.
   142	   Divergent escape, instruction-template, or section-ordering logic is
   143	   reconciled with byte-equivalence tests before the duplicate is removed.
   144	
   145	5. **Review prompt contents.** The review prompt includes, in priority
   146	   order:
   147	   1. Bead metadata + acceptance criteria + bead notes (minimum evidence
   148	      floor — always present regardless of budget).
   149	   2. Changed-file inventory (names + stat lines — minimum evidence floor).
   150	   3. Governing document content, bounded per §1. Governing docs that
   151	      exceed their cap degrade to heading outline + first N lines.
   152	   4. Diff, ranked by relevance (files named in AC text > files matching
   153	      governing ref paths > others); per-file cap applies; large files
   154	      degrade to stat + hunk headers.
   155	
   156	6. **Grading prompt contents.** The grading prompt follows the same
   157	   bounded-assembly pattern and additionally includes, bounded via
   158	   `ClampOutput`, each comparison arm's `PostRunOut`, `PostRunOK`, and
   159	   `ToolCalls`. These are first-class evidence fields on the comparison
   160	   arm type and must not be silently dropped.
   161	
   162	7. **Pre-dispatch short-circuit.** When a bounded assembly still exceeds
   163	   `MaxPromptBytes` after all trimming, the call site must not dispatch
   164	   the provider. Reviewer paths emit a distinct
   165	   `review-error: context_overflow` outcome (see §13).
   166	
   167	**Bounded prompt ingress**
   168	
   169	8. **Hard-fail on oversize `--prompt` files.** Every call site that reads a
   170	   user-supplied prompt file uses `evidence.ReadFileClamped(MaxPromptBytes)`
   171	   and hard-fails on oversize with an actionable error message that
   172	   includes observed size and cap. Silent truncation is forbidden — a
   173	   user-supplied prompt truncated without notice produces incorrect
   174	   results the user cannot diagnose.
   175	
   176	9. **Hard-fail on oversize inline prompt bodies.** The same hard-fail
   177	   applies to inline prompt bodies in `/api/agent/run` request bodies,
   178	   `DDX_AGENT_PROMPT` environment values, and any other ingress path that
   179	   accepts a raw prompt string. Size is checked against `MaxPromptBytes`
   180	   before the prompt is handed to the agent.
   181	
   182	**Bounded server text egress**
   183	
   184	10. **Universal bounded text responses.** Every server text-returning
   185	    handler — MCP tools, REST endpoints, GraphQL resolvers — that emits
   186	    library document content, execution bundle content, diff content,
   187	    session content, or persona content applies `ClampOutput` and marks
   188	    the response as truncated in the response structure when trimming
   189	    occurred. The MCP tool response structure, the REST JSON response,
   190	    and the GraphQL resolver return type each carry an explicit
   191	    `truncated: bool` and `original_bytes` field where applicable.
   192	
   193	11. **No prompt-adjacent surface is unbounded.** The invariant applies
   194	    regardless of whether a specific response is, today, actually consumed
   195	    back into a prompt. A bounded-by-default policy prevents new
   196	    consumers from reintroducing the overflow class.
   197	
   198	**Error classes and review policy**
   199	
   200	12. **Review error taxonomy.** The reviewer outcome event distinguishes
   201	    four classes:
   202	    - `review-error: context_overflow` — bounded assembly exceeded
   203	      `MaxPromptBytes` post-trim; provider was not called.
   204	    - `review-error: provider_empty` — provider returned zero bytes.
   205	    - `review-error: unparseable` — provider returned text but no
   206	      recognizable verdict line.
   207	    - `review-error: transport` — network or provider error.
   208	
   209	13. **Reviewer failure preserves the gate.** Reviewer failure must not
   210	    close the bead. This invariant (enforced today by
   211	    `execute_bead_review_failure_modes_test.go`) is preserved: bounded
   212	    prompts change how the reviewer is called, not whether a failed
   213	    review can auto-approve.
   214	
   215	14. **Bounded review retry.** Reviewer invocations are capped at a
   216	    configurable N attempts per `result_rev`. On the Nth failure, DDx
   217	    emits a terminal `review-manual-required` event and stops re-
   218	    executing primary. A new `result_rev` (fresh primary attempt after
   219	    manual operator action) resets the counter. Iterations that do not
   220	    produce a `result_rev` (e.g. `--no-merge` runs, execution-failed
   221	    outcomes) do not consume the review-retry budget; the counter is
   222	    scoped strictly to reviewer attempts against a committed result.
   223	    This bounds the cost-bleed pattern observed in the filed bug (same
   224	    primary task retried 3–4 times) without weakening the gate.
   225	
   226	**Telemetry and observability**
   227	
   228	15. **Section-level assembly telemetry.** Review and grading paths
   229	    extend the execute-bead attempt bundle (FEAT-014 §19/§20, FEAT-005
   230	    §Execute-Bead Evidence Bundle) with an evidence-assembly block
   231	    containing:
   232	    - per-section bytes (bead / governing / diff / arm-output)
   233	    - list of selected vs omitted governing refs
   234	    - list of selected vs omitted diff files
   235	    - truncation reasons per section
   236	    - total `input_bytes` and `output_bytes`
   237	    - `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, `model`
   238	
   239	    Runtime metrics fields already defined by FEAT-014 (`harness`,
   240	    `model`, `session_id`, `elapsed_ms`, `input_tokens`,
   241	    `output_tokens`, `total_tokens`, `cost_usd`, `base_rev`,
   242	    `result_rev`) are not duplicated by FEAT-022; evidence-assembly
   243	    telemetry is additive to that record, not a replacement.
   244	
   245	16. **Compact event summary.** `review`, `review-error`, and
   246	    `compare-result` event bodies carry a compact summary
   247	    (`input_bytes`, `output_bytes`, `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, `model`).
   248	    Full section detail stays in artifacts; event bodies respect the
   249	    existing size cap on bead event bodies.
   250	
   251	17. **Metrics surface.** DDx's existing review-outcomes metrics surface
   252	    exposes prompt-size quantiles and the four-class failure breakdown
   253	    defined in §12. The specific CLI and API entry points that carry
   254	    this data are inherited from FEAT-006 and FEAT-014; FEAT-022 adds
   255	    fields, not new commands.
   256	
   257	18. **Structured assembly topology.** The multi-turn structured-evidence
   258	    assembly architecture is specified in TD-033. FEAT-022 owns the
   259	    bounded prompt invariants, section ordering, and failure semantics;
   260	    TD-033 owns the caller-facing structured evidence envelope and the
   261	    no-tool reviewer-mode convention used by FEAT-006 and FEAT-008.
   262	
   263	### Non-Functional
   264	
   265	- **Lint (structural sink coverage).** A vet/lint rule, scoped
   266	  structurally not by dataflow, enforces the invariant in §3. Rule keys:
   267	  - assignment to `RunOptions.Prompt`
   268	  - construction of `mcpContent{Type:"text", ...}` literals
   269	  - server handlers that write text response bodies from library,
   270	    execution, diff, session, or persona data
   271	  - readers of `opts.PromptFile` or inline prompt-body request fields
   272	  Any flagged path must route through an `internal/evidence` primitive
   273	  or carry an explicit `evidence:allow-unbounded` annotation with a
   274	  justifying comment.
   275	
   276	- **Byte-based enforcement and calibration.** All caps are enforced in
   277	  bytes. Token-based caps are deferred until DDx has a per-harness
   278	  tokenizer binding. Default byte caps are calibrated conservatively
   279	  relative to the smallest common-reviewer model's context window using
   280	  an assumed worst-case bytes-per-token ratio; the solution design

    10	# Technical Design: Multi-Turn Structured Evidence Assembly
    11	
    12	## Purpose
    13	
    14	This TD defines the architecture for assembling large DDx prompts in a
    15	structured, multi-turn way. FEAT-022 defines the evidence caps and
    16	bounded-assembly invariants; this TD defines how callers stage evidence,
    17	resolve the assembly mode, and render a final prompt without each surface
    18	inventing its own topology.
    19	
    20	The design exists for three callers that need consistent behavior:
    21	
    22	- the agent service boundary in FEAT-006
    23	- the UI-triggered review flow in FEAT-008
    24	- the bounded review and grading prompts in FEAT-022
    25	
    26	The goal is not to introduce a single monolithic builder. The goal is to
    27	standardize the shape of the assembly process so the review path, grading
    28	path, and primary invocation path can share the same evidence vocabulary
    29	while still choosing different top-level ordering and budgets.
    30	
    31	## Current State
    32	
    33	Today FEAT-022 already requires bounded prompt assembly, minimum evidence
    34	floors, and a no-unbounded-prompts invariant. What is still missing is a
    35	documented topology for how a caller moves from raw evidence to the final
    36	rendered prompt when the request is review-like and tool-free.
    37	
    38	That gap shows up in three places:
    39	
    40	- FEAT-006 owns the request boundary but does not yet name the review-mode
    41	  assembly shape.
    42	- FEAT-008 exposes an "Agent review" action but does not yet say that the
    43	  resulting session is tool-free by convention.
    44	- FEAT-022 defines the caps and the section content rules but intentionally
    45	  stops short of mandating a repo-wide `EvidenceAssembler` object.
    46	
    47	## Design
    48	
    49	### 1. Structured evidence envelope
    50	
    51	Every assembly run begins with a small structured envelope that records:
    52	
    53	- intent: `primary`, `review`, or `grading`
    54	- mode: `ref-only` or `inline-with-cap`
    55	- target budget: the applicable cap for the chosen intent
    56	- evidence source set: bead metadata, docs, diffs, runs, or prompt bodies
    57	
    58	The envelope is not the final prompt. It is the contract that lets the caller
    59	plan the assembly without knowing the full content shape up front.
    60	
    61	### 2. Multi-turn assembly phases
    62	
    63	The assembly proceeds in three phases:
    64	
    65	1. Plan the evidence set and section priority order.
    66	2. Materialize the sections with the FEAT-022 cap primitives.
    67	3. Render the final prompt and record the byte accounting.
    68	
    69	This can happen in one process call, but it is still a multi-turn
    70	architecture conceptually because the caller first chooses the evidence plan
    71	and then performs the final render against that plan.
    72	
    73	### 3. No-tool reviewer mode
    74	
    75	Review-mode sessions do not receive tools. The caller hands the reviewer a
    76	structured evidence envelope plus the rendered prompt, and the reviewer is
    77	expected to reason over the supplied content only.
    78	
    79	That convention matters because the review prompt is meant to be stable and
    80	auditable. If a reviewer needs additional evidence, the caller must assemble it
    81	up front rather than relying on a later tool call.
    82	
    83	This is the mode FEAT-006 uses when it launches review-bearing invocations and
    84	the mode FEAT-008 exposes through its browser-driven "Review" action.
    85	
    86	### 4. Caller-specific ordering
    87	
    88	The shared envelope does not force a single assembly order across all call
    89	sites.
    90	
    91	- FEAT-022 review and grading paths keep their own section priorities.
    92	- FEAT-006 keeps request-level intent and evidence capture ownership.
    93	- FEAT-008 surfaces the review action and dispatches the session in the
    94	  no-tool reviewer mode described here.
    95	
    96	## Non-Goals
    97	
    98	- This TD does not replace FEAT-022's cap primitives.
    99	- This TD does not define new routing policy.
   100	- This TD does not introduce map-reduce per-file review or any other
   101	  parallel fan-out architecture.
   102	- This TD does not require a new repository-wide assembler type.

Document-Driven Development eXperience

DDx is a toolkit for AI-assisted development that helps you:

• Share templates, prompts, and patterns across projects
• Maintain consistent development practices
• Integrate AI tooling seamlessly
• Contribute improvements back to the community

Get started:
  ddx init          Initialize DDx in your project
  ddx list          See available resources
  ddx doctor        Check installation and diagnose issues

More information:
  Documentation: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx
  Issues & Support: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/issues

Usage:
  ddx [command]

Available Commands:
  agent       Invoke AI agents with harness dispatch, quorum, and session logging
  bead        Manage beads (portable work items)
  checkpoint  Create, list, or restore named git checkpoints
  completion  Generate completion script
  config      Configure DDx settings
  doc         Document dependency graph and staleness tracking
  doctor      Check installation health and diagnose issues
  exec        Validate and run DDx execution definitions
  help        Help about any command
  init        Initialize DDx in current project
  install     Install a package, resource, or the embedded DDx skills
  installed   List installed packages
  jq          Process JSON with jq expressions
  list        List available DDx resources
  log         Show DDX asset history

  skills      Validate bundled skill packages
  status      Show version and status information
  uninstall   Remove an installed package
  update      Update DDx CLI or installed plugins
  upgrade     Upgrade DDx to the latest version
